title: How One Mom Used Entrepreneurship to Reclaim Her Power and Transform Her Family
guestBio: Kathleen Reed is the founder of West Coast Myofunctional Therapy, a specialized practice focused on orofacial myofunctional therapy for children and adults. As a mother of three and passionate advocate for whole-family wellness, she combines her expertise in health with her entrepreneurial spirit to help families achieve better sleep, growth, and vitality.
excerpt: Discover how Kathleen Reed transformed her life through entrepreneurship and myofunctional therapy expertise. Learn practical strategies for making invisible labor visible and using technology to reclaim precious time for what truly matters in your family and business.
metaDescription: Learn how entrepreneur Kathleen Reed reclaimed her power through business ownership while balancing motherhood, wellness advocacy, and purposeful leadership.
keyTakeaways: Entrepreneurship can be a powerful tool for reclaiming personal agency and creating the life you want on your own terms.
Orofacial myofunctional therapy addresses foundational issues that impact sleep, growth, and overall family wellness.
Leadership doesn't require a fancy title—it happens in kitchens, carpool lines, and bedtime routines every day.
Technology and AI can be leveraged to streamline work processes and create more time for meaningful family moments.
Making invisible labor visible in both home and business settings is essential for sustainable success.
Faith and family values can serve as powerful guiding principles in entrepreneurial ventures.
Taking time for rest and adventure, like off-grid family experiences, is crucial for maintaining perspective and joy.

pullQuotes.text: Leadership doesn't require a fancy title—it happens in kitchens, carpool lines, and bedtime routines.

timestamps.label: How The Manage Her book transformed Kathleen's approach
Using technology and AI to reclaim time

content:
What if entrepreneurship could give you back the power you didn't even realize you'd lost? That's exactly what happened to Kathleen Reed, founder of West Coast Myofunctional Therapy, and her story will inspire you to rethink what's possible when you take control of your own narrative.

In this powerful conversation with Aimee Rickabus, Kathleen opens up about her journey from feeling stuck in traditional roles to creating a thriving business that serves families while honoring her own values and priorities. As a mother of three, stepmother, and entrepreneur, she's living proof that you don't have to choose between family and professional fulfillment.

The Power of Specialized Expertise

Kathleen's expertise in orofacial myofunctional therapy might sound technical, but its impact on families is profound and practical. This specialized field addresses the foundational patterns of breathing, swallowing, and oral posture that affect everything from sleep quality to proper growth and development in both children and adults.

Think about it: how many family challenges stem from poor sleep, behavioral issues related to breathing problems, or developmental concerns that could be addressed at their root? Kathleen saw a need in her community and had the courage to build her expertise and business around solving real problems for real families.

What's particularly inspiring about Kathleen's approach is how she's created a practice that aligns with her values of whole-family wellness. She's not just treating symptoms—she's helping families understand and address underlying issues that impact their quality of life. This is entrepreneurship with purpose, and it's exactly the kind of leadership our communities need.

Applying The Manage Her Principles at Home

After reading The Manage Her from cover to cover, Kathleen didn't just absorb the concepts—she actively applied them to transform how her household operates. The principles of making invisible labor visible, creating systems for time freedom, and leading with intention became practical tools in her daily life.

This is where the conversation gets really practical for those of us juggling multiple roles. Kathleen shares how she's brought business-level thinking to her home management, creating clarity around responsibilities and expectations that has reduced stress and increased everyone's sense of ownership in the family's success.

The key insight here? The same leadership skills that drive business success can revolutionize how your household functions. When you stop accepting chaos as inevitable and start applying intentional systems, everything shifts. Your time becomes more your own, your family members step up with greater responsibility, and you model what purposeful leadership looks like in everyday life.

Leadership Without the Title

One of the most powerful themes in this conversation is recognizing that leadership happens everywhere—not just in boardrooms or corner offices. Kathleen embodies this truth as she navigates the complex dynamics of motherhood, step-parenting, and entrepreneurship while maintaining her sense of purpose and identity.

The reality is that you're already leading, whether you recognize it or not. Every time you make a decision that prioritizes your family's wellbeing, every time you choose to build something meaningful rather than accept the status quo, every time you show up authentically in your relationships—that's leadership in action.

Kathleen's story reminds us that leadership is less about having authority over others and more about taking responsibility for the outcomes you want to see. When she recognized that traditional employment wasn't giving her the flexibility and autonomy she needed to serve her family well, she created her own solution through entrepreneurship.

Technology as a Tool for Freedom

What sets Kathleen apart is her strategic approach to using technology and AI to streamline her work and reclaim time for what matters most. Instead of being overwhelmed by the possibilities or resistant to change, she's embraced these tools as a way to serve her clients better while maintaining boundaries around her family time.

This is such an important mindset shift for busy women who often feel like technology is just one more thing to manage. When you approach it as a tool for creating more space in your life rather than more complexity, everything changes. Kathleen demonstrates how you can leverage these resources to work more efficiently, communicate more effectively, and ultimately show up more fully for both your business and your family.

The lesson here? Don't let perfectionism or fear keep you from exploring tools that could significantly improve your quality of life. Start small, experiment with what works, and remember that the goal isn't to become a tech expert—it's to create more time and mental space for the relationships and experiences that truly matter to you.

Faith, Family, and Adventure

Perhaps what's most refreshing about Kathleen's approach to life and business is how she's maintained space for rest, adventure, and spiritual grounding. Her family's off-grid van life experiences aren't just Instagram-worthy moments—they're intentional choices about what kind of memories and values she wants to prioritize.

This integration of faith, family adventure, and business success challenges the narrative that entrepreneurship requires sacrificing everything else. Instead, Kathleen shows us what it looks like to build a business that supports and enhances your desired lifestyle rather than consuming it.

The off-grid adventures serve as more than just family fun—they're a reset, a way to gain perspective on what's truly essential, and an opportunity to model for her children that life is meant to be lived fully. When you're willing to step outside conventional approaches to both business and family life, you create possibilities that others might never consider.

Your next step? Consider what adventures, values, or experiences you've been putting on hold until "someday." Kathleen's story is permission to start integrating those priorities now, not after you've achieved some arbitrary level of success or stability.

Entrepreneurship gave Kathleen her power back not because it made her wealthy or famous, but because it allowed her to align her daily actions with her deepest values. It gave her the flexibility to show up for her family, the autonomy to serve her community in meaningful ways, and the confidence that comes from creating something valuable with your own hands and mind.

That same power is available to you, whether through traditional entrepreneurship, intrapreneurship within existing organizations, or simply by applying entrepreneurial thinking to how you manage your current roles and responsibilities. The question isn't whether you have what it takes—it's whether you're ready to step into the power you already possess.

guestLinks.label: 📚 Grab your copy of The Manage Her

quiz.title: What's Your Power Reclaiming Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally take back control and create the life you want on your own terms. Find out which approach resonates most with your journey toward personal agency.

questions.question: When you feel like your life isn't reflecting who you really are, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Start researching how to launch something entirely new that excites me
Look for proven systems or frameworks I can implement step by step
Get clear on my core values and what needs to change to honor them
Consider how any changes I make will impact everyone in my family

questions.question: Your definition of 'having it all' looks like:

options.text: Building something meaningful while proving I can succeed on my own terms
Having efficient systems that let me excel at work and be present at home
Making choices that align with my beliefs, even if they're unconventional
Creating an environment where everyone in my family can thrive together

questions.question: When you discover something that could help your family, you typically:

options.text: Dive deep and consider if this could become something I offer to other families too
Research the most effective way to implement it and create a plan
Evaluate whether it fits with our family's values and long-term vision
Think about how it might address multiple issues we're facing simultaneously

questions.question: Your biggest frustration with traditional approaches to work-life balance is:

options.text: They assume you have to accept limitations instead of creating your own path
They're too vague and don't provide concrete systems that actually work
They ignore the importance of living according to your personal values
They treat work and life as separate instead of integrated parts of who you are

questions.question: When you imagine your ideal leadership style, you see yourself:

options.text: Taking charge and showing others what's possible when you refuse to settle
Leading by example through consistent actions and reliable follow-through
Inspiring others by staying true to your principles, even when it's difficult
Creating positive change that naturally spreads to benefit your whole community

questions.question: The idea that excites you most about entrepreneurship is:

options.text: Having complete control over your decisions and the direction of your life
Building efficient processes that create more time for what matters most
Creating work that reflects your values and serves a purpose beyond profit
Solving problems that make life better for families like yours

results.title: The Bold Builder
results.description: You're someone who sees what needs to change and takes decisive action to build something new. When you feel stuck, you channel that energy into creating solutions that serve both you and others. Your entrepreneurial spirit is strong, and you're not afraid to step into uncharted territory when it means living authentically. Kathleen's story of transforming her entire family's life through entrepreneurship will inspire you to trust your instincts and take that next bold step.

results.title: The Steady Transformer
results.description: You believe in the power of consistent, intentional changes that build momentum over time. Rather than dramatic shifts, you prefer to methodically address the root causes of what's not working in your life. Your approach to reclaiming power is thoughtful and sustainable, focusing on systems and foundations that create lasting change. You'll love hearing how Kathleen applied structured principles to transform her household operations and create the life her family truly wanted.

results.title: The Values-Led Visionary
results.description: Your north star is living in alignment with what matters most to you, and you're willing to make significant changes to honor those values. You understand that true power comes from making decisions that reflect your authentic self, even when others don't understand. Your leadership style is deeply personal and purpose-driven. Kathleen's journey of building a business that honors her faith and family values while creating meaningful impact will resonate deeply with your approach to life.

results.title: The Holistic Harmonizer
results.description: You see the interconnectedness of all areas of life and believe that real transformation happens when you address multiple dimensions simultaneously. You're drawn to solutions that benefit not just you, but your entire family and community. Your power comes from creating balance and wellness that ripples outward. You'll be inspired by how Kathleen's expertise in addressing foundational health issues created profound changes for entire families, proving that personal transformation can have far-reaching impact.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hey everyone, welcome back to The Manage Her. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus and today's guest is not just a powerhouse entrepreneur, she's also my friend, my neighbor, and a fellow member of our seaside sisterhood. 

Kathleen Reed is the founder of West Coast Myofunctional Therapy and is a specialist in orofacial and myofunctional therapy which provides ideal growth and development in children and whole body wellness and vitality for patients of all ages through airway sleep and digestive health support. She's a mom to 5-year-old Shepherd, a stepmom to two teenagers, and married to her amazing husband Brian. When they're not working, they're off the grid in their van reconnecting with nature and each other. She's also read The Manager cover to cover.

Today we're going to talk about the kind of leadership that doesn't always come with the title, the kind that shows up in kitchens, in carpool lines, in patient care, and in building a life that actually fits. Let's get into it.

Hey, Kathleen, welcome to The Manager. It feels so good to have you here and in person. Thanks for coming in.

**Kathleen:** It is such an honor to be here. I love your book. Oh my god. Thank you so much. I love all the work that you're doing. So I'm really glad to be here today.

**Aimee:** Thank you. Let's get into this myofunctional therapy. Let's explain it for people who have no idea what this is.

**Kathleen:** Okay. So it's actually orofacial myofunctional therapy. So orofacial is mouth face, myofunctional muscle function. So basically think of it like physical therapy for the muscles of the mouth and the face. We work on things like oral resting posture, which is how we hold our mouths at rest, proper swallowing, proper chewing, and proper breathing. So, all of our normal everyday functions, we just want to make sure that we're optimally doing them.

**Aimee:** That's so cool. And how did you get into this?

**Kathleen:** When my son was born, he was born with such a severe tongue tie that he could barely even take a bottle. My background is actually dental hygiene. I've been a dental hygienist for 20 years. I remember looking down at my son and being like, man, I don't know what a newborn baby's mouth looks like. But if you were an adult, this would be a tongue tie. We really didn't have a lot of education in that. This wasn't part of the curriculum or anything like that. But I just had a suspicion.

It was completely missed. It was missed in the hospital. It was missed by the pediatrician, the lactation consultant. I mean, just nobody really knew about it. And I suffered. I mean, I really suffered. My child couldn't eat. I wasn't sleeping. Finally, we had him assessed. We had that tongue tie released when he was 7 days old, but we were really rushed through the process and it was really traumatic for me.

Even as a hygienist, I couldn't do the stretches firmly enough. It was just a nightmare. There's definitely a way to go about doing things where the timing is correct. But then as I went on and learned more, I found out that kids that get that tongue tie release, well, it's great and it usually gets them breastfeeding, but that's not the end of the road. Most of them end up with myofunctional issues and are going to need a secondary release and myofunctional therapy down the road.

When I learned that I could do that as a dental hygienist with advanced training, I dove in. Then I found out how much it helps with sleep apnea. During this process, my husband was diagnosed with severe obstructive sleep apnea. So I just dove head first. The two loves of my life were going to benefit from something that I could learn how to do. And so I went into it.

**Aimee:** That's so cool. It's so funny how we're so inspired by our children, our husbands, and how they often inspire our careers and what we end up doing. It seems to be very hand-in-hand with the women that I've interviewed and it's very much true with you as well.

**Kathleen:** Yeah, I know Indie had her tongue tie too and so she had her... I think we did her release. We did a laser process on her when she was I think she was about 6 weeks old when we did it but it was so traumatic.

**Aimee:** Yes, it really was. And what I wanted was to make sure that no other mom went through what I went through, right? It is so true. When you have the people that you love, if a mom looking for answers for her child, there is nothing that will stop that mom.

**Kathleen:** Totally. Yeah. And now I'm noticing that she sometimes she snores, but you're right, she never had any follow-up care, so she had a really bad tongue tie and so now she can stick her tongue out really far. But I'm noticing she probably still has some airway issues, especially when she's sleeping.

**Aimee:** Exactly. And that was Shepherd, too. I mean, he has been my little learning process. That boy didn't sleep through the night until he was 4 years old after he eventually had to have his adenoids removed. I tried everything holistic that I could do. But snoring, restless sleep, and all of that was starting to impact his behavior, of course.

Children who are not sleeping well, their behavior is going to look exactly like ADHD. They're not sleeping well. They're not going to be laying down the same amount of gray matter in their brain. They're also not going to grow because during deep sleep is when human growth hormone is secreted. And so they're not going to grow well. Sleep is so important. The way a child breathes and sleeps is so important for everything.

So many of us, especially the way orthodontics were done back in the day removing teeth and just straightening teeth rather than expanding outward, all of us can really benefit from myofunctional therapy.

**Kathleen:** Absolutely. If there was a mom out there and she's like how do I know if my child needs this? Are there some really telltales that we could look at?

**Aimee:** Oh absolutely. Well snoring is a huge red flag. There's no healthy snoring in a child. They are not breathing well. They're getting low-grade oxygen deprivation all night. And not even necessarily low grade, it could be severe. I mean, you would never let somebody just put their hands around your child's neck and squeeze at night. And that's what snoring is. It's depriving them of oxygen.

Open mouth breathing. Mouth breathing in kids is a really big red flag. When you mouth breathe, you're going to put your body naturally into fight or flight because you have to breathe faster and shallower. You're not able to absorb as much oxygen as if you were nasal breathing. And so it disregulates. And adults, same thing too. You're disregulated. You will feel anxious even when there's nothing going on. But your whole body suffers. You're not getting oxygen. That is the one thing you can live the least amount of time without, right?

So many of us are going through life, not breathing well and not sleeping well. And those are the two biggest pillars of health. So I always tell people, let's look at your breathing and your sleep first before we try any other interventions. Let's take a look at those things because very often and this is what I do. I train people how to properly do these things the way we were designed.

**Kathleen:** Yeah. We all fall off track for whatever reason at any point in life and no one ever really taught us how to breathe. Right. That's the thing. It's no one ever said okay in school this is how we're going to do this. This is the proper way to do this guys.

**Aimee:** Exactly. You could get a cold and for two weeks you're breathing through your mouth and then now it becomes a habit. So, a lot of times in kids there can be a structural blockage there. So, we want to make sure that that's not the case. That's when we would pull in an ENT for an evaluation. But if it's just a habit, well, that's where I come into play.

But so many times moms are like, "My kid is mouth breathing. I don't know what to do. I keep telling them to stop." Well, there's a whole process. And that's what myofunctional therapy is. It's therapeutic exercises and activities. It's bringing awareness. And it's really awesome. So, for my son, I was... He was my youngest little guy. I put through a program and it's just been amazing to watch him thrive.

**Kathleen:** That's amazing. That's so cool. Well, it's like you're changing people's lives, the quality of their lives with your work.

**Aimee:** That's why I love it so much because I've always loved hygiene. I liked it. But myofunctional therapy really allows me to change the trajectory of people's lives in a meaningful way.

**Kathleen:** I love that. And I love how you found balance. You were working 5 days a week as a dental hygienist and not loving it, burnt out.

**Aimee:** So yeah, dental hygiene was a great career for me. This goes back to, I wanted to go to art school, but my mom said, "No, you need to find a career where you can support yourself and 2.5 children on your own if need be." And I really always wanted to be a mom. So I looked at dental hygiene like a career that would be great as a mom. I could work more when I don't have kids and less when I did. And it was really great.

But at the same time, it really did not give me that time freedom. Right. I'm basically been chained to a dental operatory for all these years and on someone else's schedule. If a dentist decides I want to change the hours up and so now we're working late on Tuesdays. Well, that's what you have to do and you're in an office.

After I had Shepherd... You remember my bed rest and all that?

**Kathleen:** Oh my gosh. So, you were on bed rest for 20 weeks with him.

**Aimee:** Yeah, for 4 months and he was born a month early and that was a really tough pregnancy and so he will be my one and only child and I just really wanted to enjoy and be present. And myofunctional therapy starting my own business has completely given me that freedom.

**Kathleen:** It is so good. Oh, it is so good. And now you can go to the dental office and clean one day a week and be your best self.

**Aimee:** Right. Yeah, exactly. It's my fun little day. I go... I still go one day a week. I see six patients, my favorite patients, and I go see my friends at work because I do still like hygiene, but it's about finding balance in your life, right? It's about finding what works for you.

**Kathleen:** Exactly. Yeah. And I think that's where women are evolving and I love to see us rising. And I think that is how we rise is by taking our time back, right?

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Autonomy over my schedule. You almost can't put a price on that. Because I am an extremely hard worker, but I also like to be able to plug things in very deliberately and intentionally so that I can be super present with my family. So, it's just really nice to have that freedom and make my own schedule.

**Kathleen:** I love it. Yeah. And I feel the same way. I mean, I've always been an entrepreneur and it's been one thing I loved about my whole life. I've been an entrepreneur since I was 22 years old. I always I couldn't trade it because I went to film school thinking, I'll go be a screenwriter and I'll go work for Disney or somebody and write screenplays. I always thought I'd write children's movies or something. And I couldn't do it because working for someone else and not being on my own schedule, it's such a deal breaker for me.

**Aimee:** It's a deal breaker. I think that entrepreneurship gives you the freedom over your own time that maybe you've never had it in your whole life. I am so free. It is just so delicious. It really is because you pick when you're going to work with your clients and you make it work around what you want to do with your life.

**Kathleen:** Exactly. And if I want to go to an event at my son's school or if I need to pick him up somewhere or I need to get him to an appointment or my husband and I want to take off on a long weekend and go camping like we do, I can plan that in. So, it's great. It's just such freedom.

What it's given me is such a sense of contentment because even when I'm home on a day doing laundry, I'm so glad because I'm getting my laundry done before the weekend rather than before when I was at the dental office. I can't be doing laundry at home, moving things along. So, I'm grateful to be doing that laundry in the middle of a day on a Wednesday.

**Aimee:** Yeah, because when you're not burnt out, it's actually fun to do those things. When you're burnt out, it's no fun to cook dinner. When you're burnt out, it's no fun to do laundry. But when you have, your cup is full, you're happy as a human being, it's a lot easier and more joyful to do cooking, grocery shopping, laundry, and all the things that we do love to do to keep our family moving. All of the not so invisible work that we do in our homes. We want to be joyful when we're doing those things. We don't want to be resentful when we're doing them.

**Kathleen:** Exactly. And that's the big shift for a lot of us who've made the shift into entrepreneurship is that when we do those things at home, I'm excited. If I'm cooking dinner for my family, I'm happy to be doing that. If I'm doing laundry, I'm happy to be doing that. And it's because I'm not burnt out, just like you said.

**Aimee:** Yeah. So, and that's the thing, too, is having control over your schedule. Well, if there's times when I need to scale back a little bit to focus on other things or just, I can do that. I don't have to be burned out. It's a choice. It's a choice to manage my own life and my own schedule in such a way that we can all flow nicely and be happy.

**Kathleen:** I love it. That's amazing. So, you're one of my first guests who's read my whole book.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Kathleen:** And I would love to get into it with you. Is there a chapter or a quote or a theme that stuck with you the most?

**Aimee:** Oh, well, I just devoured that book. I loved it so much. And as I was reading, I kept thinking to myself of all the friends I want to buy this for and give it to. And it was just so great.

Well, I think the biggest thing that I really liked about it was that you focus on it's not women are better and men are awful and all that. No, it was we just want gender parity. We just want to work as more of a team. We love our husbands. We've got great... we love our guys. We have sons. It's not about one being better than the other, but it's like, hey, let's just name some of these elephants in the room, which are the unseen labor and boundaries that need to be set and things like that and talking about it, just bringing the conversation.

**Kathleen:** Yeah. Absolutely. Yeah. And when we were talking about it, I realized we call it invisible labor, but it's really unspoken labor. It's the fact that for some reason we're not allowed to say what we're doing. All of the contributions that the women are doing in the household have to be very quietly done. They have to be very... it's like we're sneaking around like little fairies or like Santa's elves doing all this work without naming it, without getting recognition for it or having accountability.

There's no... and then I feel like with that we're doing it at the subconscious level rather than bringing it to this conscious level of the household. And we as the managers of our household I think that we have a duty to bring forward and to communicate what we're doing. We're not second-class citizens. We're not subhuman. We're very much equals in this country in this time on this planet. And it's time for us to be like, I washed the dishes. And everyone's like, that's awesome, Mom. Thank you.

**Aimee:** Well, I really loved it, too, how you provided the language for me to kind of have these conversations with my husband because thinking of yourself as the household manager and in a business sense, it's something that I really hadn't done before, but it gave me the verbiage to go back to my husband and talk about systems and standard operating procedures and how when things aren't done a certain way and how if it's done at less than 80%, it's my duty to remind people, identify it, bring them back up to speed, make sure they understand how to do it. But it's not me being difficult or... It's just operations management.

**Kathleen:** Exactly. And that's... that would make sense in a business, but I'm like, you would be fired if this was a business. I mean, honestly, it's like I've been thinking about this a lot. If my kids would just wash their own dishes. Everything would go so much more smoothly in the kitchen. And it's like just bringing an awareness. This is the new SOP. Everyone is to wash... If you use a plate or a cup, just wash it. Wash it. Put it on the drying side.

**Aimee:** Right. And that was what I realized too is that maybe I haven't been clear in describing the systems and you talk about making a little handbook for all of those over eight in your household. Great idea. What a great idea. Because again, you talk about this in the book is bringing the power back to what you have control over and owning your stuff.

So for me that was noticing, hey, maybe I haven't been as clear in communicating the system, why it's important, instead I silently get annoyed when things aren't going as planned and then that builds resentment and then you are slamming things around and frustrated. But that was me. I was like, why am I so resentful when I'm doing this? And it's because I felt like I wasn't allowed to communicate what I needed and my own personal boundaries within the household.

I realized that there wasn't anyone else telling me. I didn't need anyone else's permission to do those things. So it was like once I gave myself permission to identify what I was doing and to communicate it, it's like, oh, okay, that was it.

**Kathleen:** Exactly. And my husband is so great. He is just... Brian, I just love him so much. And he wants to do right and he wants to... there literally there's never been a time when I've communicated something to him that I need or want that he's not like oh thanks for telling me that and sure and let me try a new way.

I think so often and I watched this in my mom growing up and my mom's so awesome but I watched her always putting herself last and just working like a dog and thankless and so that was the model for me. I thought being a good woman meant basically being a martyr and not talking about those things. And it's so damaging. It's not a good look.

That's why I love you wrote this book to pave a path for the younger generation of women for and for us too to just own it because we are in a really unique time. We're not living in the 1960s or the '50s. We're now in 2025. And our partners want to be partners.

**Aimee:** Yes. And we have to teach them how to be partners because the relationships that our mothers and fathers had were not the greatest. They weren't modeling real partner behavior for us.

**Kathleen:** No. And it's like they didn't learn it from their dad's behavior and we didn't learn it from our mom's behavior. And so it's how can we clean up this old messy yuck that doesn't need to be in our relationships? Because there's a reason why a lot of those marriages ended in divorce in the '80s.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Totally. Or were just people who even if they stayed married, they were miserable, right? And that's not a way to be.

**Kathleen:** No. There's this Native American metaphor about the bird of humanity that's been flying with one wing for far too long. There's the masculine and the feminine wing. And it's like the feminine wing has just been pinned back and tied down. And the masculine wing has become violent as a result. It's overworked. And that bird can't fly. It can just fly in circles and get nowhere. And that's unfortunately we've been stuck in this rut. But no more. No more.

And especially in today's world, we're like for you and I where we have all the household rules, but we also have a business that we're running. So there needs to be a shift in what's expected of the teamwork of the household management, too.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Exactly. Because we are working outside of the homes. Being inside the home isn't our whole job anymore. It's not 1950. So if we're really in a partnership, our husbands are... when your husband comes home, if you have small children, this is also a workplace. We are working until these children go to bed. This is... you're not on vacation when you get home from work, boys. If you have small children there, the work doesn't stop when you get home. The work doesn't stop till they get in bed, right? I mean, mom doesn't stop until you get everyone tucked in.

**Kathleen:** Exactly. And then even then you're tidying up the house and you're getting things ready for tomorrow. And I feel like as a mom in the house, you're always preparing. You spend half your time preparing for the next thing or getting things ready for the next thing. And that is invisible labor too. So that the morning runs smoothly because the night before you... after your husband might have turned in for the night and you're still screwing around getting the paper into the notebook and the lunches made and things like that.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Yeah. Because if you're good at managing your household, it's all about being prepared. Being prepared is the big thing for a really strong household manager. If you're a good mom, you're usually like, "Okay, I know exactly what's happening tomorrow. And if I do this, this, this, and this right now, tomorrow's going to be so much smoother."

But it's this non... it really is the non-stop carry. We're always carrying the knowledge of what's about to happen with all of the schedules. Tomorrow Shepherd has a dentist appointment. He's got to be at school at this time. He needs his lunch, his socks. Does he have clean clothes? Does he have clean socks? Where are his shoes? Can I find them easily?

**Kathleen:** Yeah, exactly. And then with a blended family, it's even more moving parts because you have kids going back and forth between households.

**Aimee:** Right. Yeah. Totally. So you're like, "Okay, we've got the kids here. We've got the kids not here. Do they have everything?"

**Kathleen:** Yes. Yeah. It's just constant work and it's the planning and the remembering everything. I mean, it's even remembering my sister-in-law's birthday card. It's just things like that where you're constantly thinking about.

**Aimee:** Totally. Yeah. And you can't forget your parents' birthdays. I'm always because they're not local. So it's like, did I get it out? Do I got to get the cards out in time? All the little things.

**Kathleen:** Yeah. All the things. Can you share how your faith has shaped your journey as a mother and as a businesswoman and how it has influenced the way that you make decisions and navigate challenges in both roles?

I think motherhood is the initial moment to surrender from the minute you get pregnant. You have to realize you're like oh my goodness I'm not in control. But I use my faith in everything. In the morning, the first thing I do is get in child's pose and I put my head down and I just ask God to come with me to go before me in all that I do today to give me the wisdom to give me the words to bring the right people in my life and to let the wrong ones fall away.
